comments.text: We have obtained an estimate for $f_r(N)$, developed in conjunction with ChatGPT-5.5 Thinking, where I worked on the argument with sourish and kireet. Our main estimate is: $$ f_r(N) \geq \exp\left((\log(r-1)+o(1))\frac{\log N}{\log\log N}\right) $$ and $$ f_r(N) \leq \exp\left(O_r\left(\frac{\log N\log\log\log N}{\log\log N}\right)\right). $$ In particular, $$ f_r(N)=N^{o(1)}. $$ The key idea is to encode each integer $n$ by the set of its prime-power divisibility layers, $$ S(n)=\{(p,j):1\leq j\leq v_p(n)\}. $$ Then $$ S(a)\cap S(b)=S(\gcd(a,b)), $$ so an $r$-tuple whose pairwise gcds are all equal is exactly an $r$-sunflower in the corresponding set system. The lower bound comes from a block construction: partition the primes into blocks of size $r-1$, and choose one prime from each block. The pigeonhole principle prevents $r$ distinct chosen products from having all pairwise gcds equal. For the upper bound, we split each integer into its smooth part and rough part. Rankin’s estimate controls the number of possible smooth parts, while the improved sunflower lemma controls each rough fiber, giving the stated upper bound. The paper is available here.

Congrats! Standard check found no mathematical issues. However, there was a literature issue. It would seem that the ALWZ citation did not contain the required result. Instead, the result could be found in this paper by Bell-Chueluecha-Warnke.

Thanks! You're right: the ALWZ citation alone does not give the exact bound used in the lemma. I revised the paper to cite Bell-Chueluecha-Warnke, Theorem 1, and added the short padding argument passing from \(K\)-element set systems to sets of size at most \(K\). Here is the revised version.
Nice! Would a positive solution to #20 remove the final $\log\log\log N$ factor in the upper bound?

Yes, for fixed $r$, a positive answer to #20 with $k=r$ would replace the $(C_r\log K)^K$ sunflower bound in Lemma 4.1 by $c_r^K$, after the same padding argument from sets of size at most $K$ to $K$-uniform sets. In the proof, $K=\lfloor \log N/\log\log N\rfloor$, and the number of possible $\log N$-smooth parts is still only\[ \exp\!\left(O\!\left(\frac{\log N}{\log\log N}\right)\right). \]So the fiber bound would become $c_r^K$, giving\[ f_r(N)\leq \exp\!\left(O_r\!\left(\frac{\log N}{\log\log N}\right)\right), \]which removes the extra $\log\log\log N$ factor. This would match the lower bound at the right exponential scale, up to the constant in the exponent. Edit: Typo.
This fact (that an exponential upper bound for the sunflower problem [20] implies the upper bound $N^{c/\log\log N}$) was already recorded in the remarks, and is noted by Erdős in [Er64].

Thanks! While it is good to have the detailed calculations written down, this is not new; it is precisely the argument sketched by Erdős in [Er64]. The improved upper bound is an immediate consequence of the improved bounds for the sunflower problem now available, when inserted into Erdős' argument.

I looked through the literature after my earlier comment, and I think the right correction is stronger than just adding a squarefree hypothesis. The counterexample $A_m=\{2,4,8,\dots,2^m\}$ shows that the current auxiliary formulation with $\omega(n)=k$ is false: for $k=1$, this family can be arbitrarily large while still avoiding an $r$-tuple with constant pairwise gcd. What seems to be the correct fix is the one Erdős gives later. In [Er73], he says that Abbott pointed out the ordinary sunflower conjecture does not seem to suffice for deriving the conjectured upper bound for $f_3(x)$. He then replaces it by a slightly stronger conjecture in which the integers have exactly $n$ prime factors counted with multiplicity. In modern notation, this is $\Omega(n)=k$, not $\omega(n)=k$. So I think the auxiliary statement here should be updated to: estimate the size of the largest set $A$ of integers such that $\Omega(n)=k$ for all $n\in A$, and there do not exist $a_1,\dots,a_r\in A$ and an integer $d$ such that $(a_i,a_j)=d$ for all $i\ne j$ and $\left(\frac{a_i}{d},d\right)=1$ for all $i$. The squarefree / $\omega(n)=k$ version is still a natural special case, and it is suggested by the argument in [Er64], but it is not the full corrected auxiliary statement that Erdős later endorsed after Abbott’s objection.

I tried to formalize the “stronger sunflower problem” phrasing here: https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/pull/2199 But as stated, this seems false without an extra restriction: for $k=1$ consider $A_m={2,4,8,\dots,2^m}.$ Then $\omega(n)=1$ for all $n\in A_m$, and for any $r\ge 3$ there is no $r$-subset with constant pairwise gcd (pairwise gcds vary with the minimal exponent), so it satisfies the “no constant pairwise gcd with coprime quotients” condition. Yet $|A_m|=m$ is arbitrarily large, contradicting any bound $|A|\le c_r^k$ (or even $c_r^k k!$ since $k=1$). In Erdős’s 1964 paper, the set-system/sunflower step is applied to squarefree parts / prime-factor sets (he explicitly introduces squarefree numbers and decomposes $a_i=A_iB_i$ with $B_i$ squarefree). That suggests the intended sunflower-style statement meant to restrict to squarefree integers (or radicals) rather than just $\omega(n)=k$? (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
